editableNewDialogue.text: I’m making a short STEAM activity sheet—can you suggest three quick projects using everyday supplies?

1) Paper bridge challenge: paper, tape, coins—build a bridge that holds the most weight.
2) Invisible ink art: white crayon/candle + watercolor—draw, then paint to reveal.
3) Marshmallow tower: marshmallows + spaghetti/toothpicks—build the tallest stable structure. Want age range and time limit?
Can you rewrite those with one-sentence instructions and one safety note each?

1) Paper Bridge: Fold and tape paper into a bridge and test it by adding coins one at a time; Safety: keep coins away from very young children who might mouth them.
2) Invisible Ink Art: Draw with a white crayon (or candle) and brush watercolor over it to reveal the picture; Safety: if using a candle, an adult should handle it and keep flames away from paper.
3) Marshmallow Tower: Use marshmallows and spaghetti/toothpicks to connect pieces and build the tallest tower you can; Safety: watch for sharp toothpick ends and dispose of broken pieces.
Also, toss in a fun fact for the sheet.
Fun fact: Spaghetti acts like a tiny column—when you bundle strands together, the structure can carry much more weight than a single strand.
Also, if you mention anything like fish oil, it has to be chicken-flavored—she turns her nose up at the plain kind.

Got it—if you try omega-3s, choosing a chicken-flavored option can make it much easier. You can also ask your vet about kitten-safe formulas and start with a tiny amount mixed into food to see if she accepts it.
About that earlier question—it's not my kitten. I’m fostering a kitten right now and her nails are weak, so I was asking what supplements might help.

Got it—so you’re fostering a kitten (not owning one). For weak nails, it’s best to check with a vet first, but they may suggest a balanced kitten diet plus options like omega-3s or biotin if appropriate.
